title: Geometric Bounds on the Finite-Time Performance of Active Machines
abstract: Optimizing energy conversion in active matter remains a central challenge in nonequilibrium physics. Here, we develop a unified thermodynamic framework that characterizes the finite-time performance of interacting active machines. We show that cyclic work admits a geometric decomposition into an antisymmetric thermodynamic curvature, governing work extraction, and a symmetric metric, controlling dissipation. Minimal-dissipation protocols follow geodesics in parameter space, while optimal work extraction deviates from them due to a curvature-induced, Lorentz-like effect. This geometric structure directly determines the finite-time scaling of work and dissipation, enabling a mapping onto Onsager-type quasi-linear current--force relations. We show that both the maximal efficiency and the efficiency at maximum power are governed by an asymmetry parameter and a figure of merit, establishing a formal correspondence between active machines and thermoelectric devices with broken time-reversal symmetry. Our results reveal a fundamental geometric origin of energy-conversion performance and provide a general framework for optimizing active machines.
summary: This work introduces a geometric thermodynamic framework to analyze how active matter machines convert energy over finite time scales. It shows that the system's performance is governed by a decomposition of work into a curvature term (work extraction) and a metric term (dissipation). This establishes a fundamental, geometric link between active matter physics and established concepts like thermoelectric efficiency.
main_problem: The paper develops a unified thermodynamic framework to characterize the finite-time performance of interacting active machines operating far from equilibrium.
main_result: It establishes that the performance bounds (efficiency and power) of active machines share the functional form of thermoelectric devices with broken time-reversal symmetry, linking active matter geometry to energy conversion limits.
method: The analysis uses stochastic thermodynamics, linear response theory, and geometric decomposition, showing that work decomposes into an antisymmetric curvature term and a symmetric dissipation metric.
model_or_system: The model involves $N$ interacting overdamped self-propelled particles (active matter) driven by external protocols ($\lambda(t)$) and subject to thermal noise. The dynamics are analyzed using Langevin equations.
key_observables: Cyclic work ($W$), heat ($Q$), power ($P$), efficiency ($\varepsilon$), asymmetry parameter ($r$), and the figure of merit ($\phi$).
important_parameters: The asymmetry parameter ($r$), the protocol shape ($C$), and the time scale ($	au$) are crucial for determining performance bounds.
main_assumptions: The analysis relies heavily on the slow-driving and weak-driving limits, utilizing linear response theory approximations.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate optimal driving cycles in parameter space, showing how increasing persistence shrinks optimal cycles toward weakly dissipative regions.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first defining the thermodynamic framework, decomposing work into geometric components, deriving scaling relations for work and heat using linear response, and finally applying these results to determine maximal efficiency and power output.
why_interesting: The geometric framework linking work extraction to curvature, and the direct analogy drawn to thermoelectric devices, provides powerful, general tools for analyzing non-equilibrium energy conversion in complex systems, which is relevant to open quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Optimal driving cycles in parameter space. Throughout the simulations, we set γ = 1, T = 1, and v = 1. Panels (a) and (c) show the optimal protocols maximizing the output power and efficiency, respectively, for different persistence times τp. The circular markers denote the initial points of the cycles, while the arrows indicate the driving directions. The protocol duration is fixed at τ = 20. As τp increases, the optimal cycles progressively shrink toward weakly dissipative regions, reflecting the increasing energetic cost associated with irreversible driving.
